text: The patient is positioned supine with legs split, and six laparoscopic ports are visibly placed on the abdomen.
The surgeon creates a window into the lesser sac and mobilizes the colon, carefully preserving the gastroepiploic artery. The hepatic flexure of the colon is dissected and taken down.
The surgeon bluntly separates the attachments of the transverse colon mesentery from the duodenum, exposing the duodenum in the surgical field.
The transverse colon is mobilized to the right and left of the gastrocolic trunk, facilitating its exposure. The surgeon ligates and divides the gastrocolic trunk of Henle.
The surgeon creates a window anterior to the hepatoduodenal ligament to facilitate the identification of the upper edge of the duodenum, with the pylorus clearly visible.

The surgeon places a stapler approximately two to three centimeters distal to the duodenum, facilitated by prior dissection between the duodenum and the head of the pancreas. The hepatoduodenal ligament is then addressed, with the gastroduodenal artery being ligated and divided. The hepatic artery is exposed and identified.

The surgeon excises the anterior hepatic artery lymph node, enhancing the visibility of the hepatic artery and the origin of the gastroduodenal artery. A frozen section of the lymph node may be performed.

The surgeon exposes the window between the hepatic artery and the gastroduodenal artery, visualizing the portal vein at the superior edge of the pancreas within the hepatoduodenal ligament. A plane is developed around the gastroduodenal artery, and a silk suture is passed through the area.

The surgeon ligates and divides the artery while carefully avoiding injury to the hepatic artery. The surgical field then shifts to entering the plane between the portal vein and the common bile duct.

The surgeon performs a 360-degree dissection of the common bile duct, with the option to ligate and divide it at this stage. The dissection progresses towards the cystic duct and gallbladder, clearly exposing the cystic duct and Calot's triangle.

The surgeon focuses on separating the neck of the pancreas from the superior mesenteric portal vein trunk. The inferior edge of the pancreas is identified, and the serosal layer overlaying this edge is opened, exposing the area where the superior mesenteric vein is connected to the pancreas.

The surgeon creates an initial window inferiorly using careful blunt dissection, which is then enlarged on both sides for direct visualization. Small vessels are ligated with ultrasonic shears to ensure a bloodless field. Once the plane is developed, finger-type dissection is used to complete the 360-degree dissection of the pancreatic neck.

The surgeon focuses on the ligament of Treitz, approaching it from the left side to remove adhesions. This is followed by a Kocher maneuver and division of the jejunum, which involves mobilizing the duodenum and jejunum for further dissection.

The third portion of the duodenum is exposed and retracted anteriorly. A window is created to divide the posterior serosal attachments to the retroperitoneum, proceeding from caudal to cephalad. The serosal layer is sharply divided, followed by careful blunt dissection to separate the retroperitoneal attachments. The inferior vena cava is exposed, and posterior attachments between the head of the pancreas and the retroperitoneum are divided.

The surgeon identifies and divides the attachments of the third and fourth portions of the duodenum located behind the superior mesenteric vessels. The duodenum is traced towards the ligament of Treitz from the right side, which is then taken down. The jejunum is identified and maneuvered behind the superior mesenteric vessels, revealing the spatial relationship between the superior mesenteric vein and the final attachments.

The surgeon delivers the jejunum to the right and creates a window at a chosen site for its division. A stapler is placed, aligning and dividing the jejunum. The pancreatic neck, previously separated from the superior mesenteric vein portal vein trunk, is divided using ultrasonic shears, providing hemostasis and a clear transection line. The splenic vein is identified.
The surgeon continues the dissection towards the area where the pancreatic duct is located. The pancreatic duct is clearly identified and then divided using scissors.

The pancreatic duct is identified and cut with scissors slightly to the right of the parenchymal transection line to aid in anastomosis. Ultrasonic shears are used to complete the transection of the pancreatic parenchyma. The supremacentric portal vein trunk is visible. The common bile duct is encircled with a bulldog clamp and divided, with the anterior wall opened first and the posterior wall cut slightly longer to facilitate future anastomosis.

The surgeon performs dissection of the uncinate process, obtaining vascular control by passing a vessel loop around the portal vein above the pancreas. The superior mesenteric vein is identified and surrounded at 360 degrees with a vessel loop for potential vascular control. The splenic vein-portal vein junction is exposed, and the splenic vein is similarly surrounded with a vessel loop.

The surgeon begins dissection of the uncinate process using a combination of bipolar and ultrasonic shear dissection. Small branch vessels between the superior mesenteric vessels and the uncinate process are controlled with bipolar dissection. The surgeon retracts the uncinate process to the right while the assistant retracts the superior mesenteric vein and artery to the left, allowing clear visualization of the superior mesenteric artery. Larger branches between the superior mesenteric portal vein trunk and the uncinate process are ligated with clips before division. Laparoscopic magnification aids in the visualization of the extensions of the uncinate process between the superior mesenteric vein and artery for complete excision.

The surgeon uses ultrasonic shears for finer dissection posterior to the superior mesenteric artery. Laparoscopic exposure provides excellent visualization, allowing the surgeon to create tension with right-sided retraction and follow lymph nodes behind the superior mesenteric vessels, which are included on the specimen side. The specimen is completely separated, with the superior mesenteric artery and vein clearly visible.

The surgeon identifies and excises any remaining fibrolymphatic tissue attached to the superior mesenteric artery. An oncologic dissection of the uncinate process is completed, and the specimen is placed in an endoscopic retrieval bag. The specimen is then removed from the abdominal cavity through an enlarged incision site.

The surgeon begins an end-to-side biliary-enteric anastomosis with the patient in a supine split-leg position. Using right-sided ports for triangulation, the proximal end of the divided jejunum is positioned behind the superior mesenteric vessels, ensuring no kinks or tension. The bulldog clamp on the bile duct is removed, and an opening in the jejunum is made to match the duct size. The seromuscular layer is opened, and the mucosa is everted. The anastomosis starts with an in-to-out stitch on the bowel and an out-to-in stitch on the duct, aligning the corners while avoiding tension. A three-millimeter tip needle driver is used for precise stitch placement, aided by magnification. The posterior layer is completed with careful suture tension management.

The surgeon begins the anterior layer of the biliary anastomosis with an outside-in, inside-out stitch, ensuring alignment with the posterior layer. The assistant maintains tension on the suture for exposure. The completed anastomosis is shown without tension. The surgeon then constructs a two-layer ductal mucosa pancreaticojejunostomy, starting with a posterior layer between the pancreas and jejunum, using a running monofilament suture. The pancreatic duct is probed with a pediatric feeding tube, and an opening is created in the jejunum using ultrasonic energy. Interrupted stitches are placed for the duct-to-mucosa anastomosis, ensuring direct contact between the mucosa of the duct and bowel. The anterior layer is completed with a running suture, inverting the pancreatic edge and covering it with the seromuscular layer of the jejunum.

A closed suction drain is positioned near the anastomosis site without direct contact. The surgeon prepares for gastrointestinal reconstruction, confirming good vascular supply around the pylorus and proximal duodenum. Anchoring stitches are placed at the ligament of Treitz area, and a site in the jejunum is selected for an antecolic postpyloric duodenal jejunostomy. The posterior seromuscular layer is created between the jejunum and duodenum edge using a running absorbable suture. The duodenum is opened longitudinally, and the jejunum is opened parallel but smaller. A 360-degree anastomosis is formed with two running sutures, incorporating all bowel layers. The anterior layer is completed with smaller bites, and an anterior inverting seromuscular stitch ensures a tension-free anastomosis. The procedure concludes with the display of port sites.
